channel.title: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium
channel.description: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium

image.title: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium

item.title: [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

If you have been following along with this tutorial, you should have:

  • Identified which key (primary or secondary) is to be updated via $(activeKey) and $(cycleKey).
  • Updated the cycle key for each of your services.
  • Update each of your access tokens, connection strings, secrets, etc.

Once you have determine the keys that you would like to cycle, you are now ready to:

  • Update your active key — because your application should now be dependant on your newly cycled key.
  • Propagate your keys to your application (e.g.: via kubernetes).

Updating your active key

So that you can keep track of which key your application is using, you will need to update your active key secret in key vault.

parameters:
  - name: azureSubscription
    type: string

  # The name of the key vault where the active key is being stored
  - name: activeKeyVaultName
    type: string
  
  # The name of the secret in the key vault (above) of the active key
  - name: activeKeySecretName
    type: string
    default: active-key

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: "set active key"
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
      scriptType: ps
      scriptLocation: inlineScript
      inlineScript: |
        az keyvault secret set `
          --output none `
          --vault-name "${{ parameters.activeKeyVaultName }}" `
          --name "${{ parameters.activeKeySecretName }}" `
          --value "$(cycleKey)" `
          --content-type "string"

Propagating to your application

The remainder of this article will focus on how to propagate your key changes to an application running on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).

Consuming Key Vault secrets from Kubernetes

I have written another four-part article which describes the process of Injecting environment variables from App Configuration and Key Vault into AKS containers. Part three specifically discusses Azure Key Vault.

Using this process, you can remove the fetching of secrets out of your application — keeping your environment configuration separate from your application code.

Automatically deploying updated keys

Consider using Reloader, it’s a handy controller that watches your Secrets (from Azure Key Vault) and ConfigMaps (from Azure App Configuration). When a secret changes (say, from a key rotation) it automatically triggers a rolling restart of any deployments that it is consuming. With this setup:

  • Your pipeline updates the secret in Azure Key Vault.
  • Reloader picks up the change by polling Azure Key Vault periodically (e.g. every minute).
  • Reloader restarts your deployment automatically and uses the new key(s).

It’s a slick way to avoid manual intervention while keeping your infrastructure secure and up-to-date.

Firstly, install Reloader via helm charts using the instructions provided in GitHub. You can then configure each of your Kubernetes deployments to each of the Kubernetes secrets (and optionally, config maps) that contain your application secrets.

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: # DeploymentName
  annotations:
    configmap.reloader.stakater.com/reload: KubernetesConfigMapName
    secret.reloader.stakater.com/reload: KubernetesSecretName

spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: # DeploymentName
# ... etc

Final steps

You should now have all the tools required to cycle keys in your Azure services automatically via an Azure DevOps pipeline and propagate the changes to your Kubernetes services.

While there is no standard recommendation for the frequency that you rotate your keys, many providers recommend you update your keys every 90 days. With the system, you can cycle your keys every day, week, month, whatever you require.

item.title: [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime.
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

If you have been following this article, you should now have two pipeline variables that you can use to help you cycle your keys:

  • The $(activeKey) which identifies which key is currently in use.
  • The $(cycleKey) which is the key to be updated.

Both should have the value of either “primary” or “secondary”.

Now comes the fun part — each Azure service cycles keys in a different way, and while there are usually only two keys not all services have the same identifier for the primary and secondary key.

Structuring the cycling of your keys

For each Azure service, you will need to create a structure similar to the following:

  • Identify the way in which primary and secondary keys are used by the specific Azure Service via the documentation.
  • Cycle (or regenerate) only the $(cycleKey).
  • Save the updated key to the key vault for use by your Kubernetes services.

Cycling keys for Azure Services

This article will give you some examples of how to cycle keys for a limited number of Azure services, you will need to read the Azure documentation for a specific service to identify how those keys are cycled.

Azure Cache for Redis

Remember when I said that each service has their own identifiers for primary and secondary keys? In Azure Cache for Redis, this is Primary and Secondary — but in the output it’s named primaryKey and secondaryKey.

Read the documentation for Regenerating Keys for Azure Cache for Redis and then check out this practical example:

---
parameters:
  # enum of either "primary" or "secondary"
  - name: cycleKey
    type: string

  - name: azureSubscription
    type: string

  - name: resourceGroup
    type: string

  # the name of the redis service you wish to cycle keys for
  - name: redisName
    type: string

  # the key vault which contains the secrets
  - name: keyVaultName
    type: string

  # an array of secrets that should contain the most up-to-date key
  - name: keyVaultSecrets
    type: object # string[]

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: "redis: ${{ parameters.redisName }}"
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
      scriptType: ps
      scriptLocation: inlineScript
      inlineScript: |
        $CYCLE_NAME = If ("$(CycleKey)" -eq "primary") { 'Primary' } Else { 'Secondary' }
        $CYCLE_INDEX = If ("$(CycleKey)" -eq "primary") { 'primaryKey' } Else { 'secondaryKey' }
        $CYCLE_VALUE = $(az redis regenerate-keys `
          --output tsv --query $CYCLE_INDEX `
          --resource-group "${{ parameters.resourceGroup }}" `
          --name "${{ parameters.redisName }}" `
          --key-type ${CYCLE_NAME}
        )
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=cycleValue;issecret=true]${CYCLE_VALUE}"

  - ${{ each secret in parameters.keyVaultSecrets  }}:
      - task: AzureCLI@2
        displayName: "↳ secret: ${{ secret }}"
        inputs:
          azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
          scriptType: ps
          scriptLocation: inlineScript
          inlineScript: |
            az keyvault secret set `
              --vault-name "${{ parameters.keyVaultName }}" `
              --name "${{ secret }}" `
              --value "$(cycleValue)" `
              --content-type "string"

Breaking down each task:

  • It maps primary and secondary to the argument of key type used to identify which key is requested to be regenerated — either Primary or Secondary.
  • It maps primary and secondary to the response of the Azure CLI request to get the primaryKey or secondaryKey representing the changed value.
  • It regenerates the requested key and stores the value in a pipeline variable named $(cycleValue).
  • The last task loops through all the specified secrets where the keys are being used and stores the new key in those key vault secrets.

Other Azure services

There are so many Azure services which has keys and connection strings — writing code for each of them would be unreasonable, so here are some links to the Azure CLI commands you will need for some commonly used services.

  • Cognitive Services (OpenAI)
    az cognitiveservices account keys regenerate
    takes Key1 and Key2
    returns key1 and key2.
  • Cosmos DB
    az cosmosdb keys regenerate
    takes primary, secondary, primaryReadonly and secondaryReadonly
    returns primaryMasterKey, secondaryMasterKey, primaryReadonlyMasterKey, secondaryReadonlyMasterKey.
  • EventHub
    az eventhubs namespace authorization-rule keys renew
    takes PrimaryKey and SecondaryKey
    returns primaryConnectionString and secondaryConnectionString.
  • IoT Hub
    az iot hub policy renew-key
    takes primary and secondary
    returns primaryKey and secondaryKey.
  • Storage accounts
    az storage account keys renew
    takes key1 and key2
    returns an array with indexed values for the output.

As you can see, there is little consistency between each of the services. You will need to check the documentation to find the appropriate commands and properties.

What about passwords?

Services like PostgreSQL don’t use keys for access control, instead you access the service using a password.

Hopefully you’re not using the superuser in your environments and you’ll have a separate user for connecting to your database. But instead of having just one user, you’ll want to manage two separate user accounts (e.g.: a primary and secondary user), then you’ll need to:

  • Generate a new password in the pipeline.
  • Connect to the PostreSQL service.
  • Update the password with an SQL statement.

Now that you know how to cycle your keys, you can continue to read how to update your active key and propagate the keys to your application.

Part 4 of 4: Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.
